title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 3
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
tradition: Hindu (Vaishnava; Bhakti, with strong Sāṅkhya influence)
date_estimate: c. 9th-10th c. CE (whole work; some elements as early as 6th c.; disputed)
summary: The third book (skandha) of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, framed largely as a dialogue between Vidura and the sage Maitreya. It expounds the cosmogony of the universe out of the un-manifest, the appearance of the Puruṣa, and the secondary creation, culminating in the celebrated discourse of the avatāra Kapila to his mother Devahūti on Sāṅkhya philosophy and devotion (bhakti-yoga).
history: The Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, one of the most influential and theologically refined of the Mahāpurāṇas, is traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa but is generally regarded by scholars as a South Indian composition of roughly the 9th-10th century CE. Canto 3 is notable for integrating classical Sāṅkhya into a devotional Vaishnava frame and attracted major commentaries such as Śrīdhara Svāmin's Bhāvārtha-dīpikā.

title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 4
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: c. 9th-10th c. CE (whole work; disputed, possibly from 6th c.)
summary: The fourth book of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, continuing the theme of secondary creation through genealogies and exemplary narratives. It recounts the story of Dakṣa and his sacrifice, the destruction of the sacrifice after Satī's self-immolation, the devotion of the child Dhruva, the righteous king Pṛthu, and the allegory of King Purañjana illustrating the soul's bondage in the body.
history: Part of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa but scholarly dated to roughly the 9th-10th century CE in South India. Canto 4's exemplary tales (Dhruva, Pṛthu) became staples of Vaishnava devotional teaching and were widely commented on, notably by Śrīdhara Svāmin.

title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 5
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: c. 9th-10th c. CE (whole work; disputed)
summary: The fifth book of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, containing the story of the renunciant king Ṛṣabha and the avadhūta-sage Jaḍa Bharata, followed by the work's detailed cosmography (bhuvana-kośa): the structure of the earth-disc (Jambūdvīpa and the surrounding continents), the heavenly bodies, the subterranean realms (pātāla), and the various hells (naraka).
history: Part of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, scholarly dated to roughly the 9th-10th century CE. Canto 5 is the principal repository of the Purāṇa's traditional Hindu cosmology and geography and is frequently cited in later cosmographical literature.

title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 6
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: c. 9th-10th c. CE (whole work; disputed)
summary: The sixth book of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, emphasizing the redemptive power of devotion and the divine name. It includes the story of the sinful Ajāmila saved by uttering 'Nārāyaṇa' at death, the protective Nārāyaṇa-kavaca hymn, the curse of Dakṣa, the birth of the Maruts, and the cosmic battle in which the gods slay the demon Vṛtra with the aid of the sage Dadhīci's bones.
history: Part of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, scholarly dated to roughly the 9th-10th century CE. The Ajāmila episode and Nārāyaṇa-kavaca became central proof-texts in Vaishnava theology of nāma-bhakti (devotion through the divine name).

title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 7
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: c. 9th-10th c. CE (whole work; disputed)
summary: The seventh book of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, centered on the story of the demon-king Hiraṇyakaśipu, his devout son Prahlāda, and Viṣṇu's incarnation as Narasiṃha (the man-lion) who tears the tyrant apart to protect the devotee. The canto also includes extended teaching on bhakti and on the duties of the varṇas and āśramas (varṇāśrama-dharma).
history: Part of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, scholarly dated to roughly the 9th-10th century CE. The Prahlāda-Narasiṃha narrative is among the most celebrated of all bhakti stories and underlies the popular Holi/Holika festival traditions.

title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 8
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: c. 9th-10th c. CE (whole work; disputed)
summary: The eighth book of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, organized around the successive Manus (manvantaras) and several of Viṣṇu's incarnations. It contains the rescue of the elephant Gajendra, the churning of the ocean (samudra-manthana) yielding the nectar of immortality and the Mohinī episode, and the story of the dwarf avatāra Vāmana who subdues the generous demon-king Bali.
history: Part of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, scholarly dated to roughly the 9th-10th century CE. The Gajendra-mokṣa prayer and the churning-of-the-ocean myth from this canto are among the most widely recited and depicted episodes in Vaishnava tradition.

title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 9
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: c. 9th-10th c. CE (whole work; disputed)
summary: The ninth book of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, presenting the royal genealogies of the Solar (sūrya-vaṃśa) and Lunar (candra-vaṃśa) dynasties. It narrates kings and sages such as Sudyumna, Saubhari, Sagara, and Rāma (a condensed Rāma-kathā), tracing the lineages down to the Yadu dynasty and thereby setting the stage for the advent of Kṛṣṇa in the following canto.
history: Part of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, scholarly dated to roughly the 9th-10th century CE. The canto functions as the dynastic-historical bridge (vaṃśānucarita) leading to the Kṛṣṇa narrative of Canto 10.

title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 10
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: c. 9th-10th c. CE (whole work; disputed)
summary: The tenth and longest book of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, devoted entirely to the life of Kṛṣṇa: his birth in Mathurā, childhood in Vraja, the subduing of demons, the lifting of Mount Govardhana, the love-play (rāsa-līlā) with the gopīs, the slaying of Kaṃsa, and his later career as prince of Dvārakā. It is the theological and devotional heart of the whole Purāṇa.
history: Part of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, scholarly dated to roughly the 9th-10th century CE. Canto 10 is the single most influential source for Kṛṣṇa-bhakti, foundational to Vaishnava movements such as Gauḍīya, Vallabha, and Nimbārka traditions, and the subject of innumerable commentaries and vernacular renderings.

title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 11
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: c. 9th-10th c. CE (whole work; disputed)
summary: The eleventh book of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, recounting the destruction of the Yadu dynasty through a brahmin curse and Kṛṣṇa's final teachings before his departure from the world. Its core is the Uddhava-gītā, an extended philosophical and devotional discourse by Kṛṣṇa to his friend Uddhava on jñāna, bhakti, yoga, and renunciation, often regarded as a counterpart to the Bhagavad-gītā.
history: Part of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, scholarly dated to roughly the 9th-10th century CE. The Uddhava-gītā (also called Haṃsa-gītā) of this canto is studied as an independent devotional-philosophical text within Vaishnava traditions.

title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 12
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: c. 9th-10th c. CE (whole work; disputed)
summary: The twelfth and final book of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, prophesying the degeneration of dharma in the Kali Yuga and describing future dynasties, the periodic dissolutions (pralaya) of the universe, and the death of King Parīkṣit by snakebite. It closes the frame narrative, gives an account of the divisions of the Vedas and the Purāṇas, and offers a concluding summary glorifying the text itself.
history: Part of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa, scholarly dated to roughly the 9th-10th century CE. The Kali-yuga prophecies of this canto are frequently quoted in discussions of Hindu eschatology.

title: Bhāgavata-Purāṇa 10, 29–33
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: c. 9th-10th c. CE (whole work; disputed)
summary: Chapters 29-33 of the tenth book of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa, known as the Rāsa-pañcādhyāyī ('five chapters on the rāsa dance'). They narrate Kṛṣṇa's autumn-night circle-dance of divine love (rāsa-līlā) with the cowherd women (gopīs) of Vraja, including their longing during his disappearance and their lament, the Gopī-gīta. This passage is widely treated as the devotional and theological climax of the entire Purāṇa.
history: An excerpt of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa Canto 10, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa and scholarly dated to roughly the 9th-10th century CE. The Rāsa-pañcādhyāyī has attracted dense allegorical and theological commentary from Vaishnava ācāryas (e.g. Śrīdhara, the Gauḍīya commentators), who read the gopīs' love as the supreme model of devotion.

title: Nīlamata-Purāṇa
author: anonymous/traditional
tradition: Hindu (regional; Naga and Shaiva-Vaishnava cults of Kashmir)
date_estimate: c. 6th-8th c. CE (with later additions; not after c. 1200 CE)
summary: A regional Purāṇa of Kashmir presented as the sage Bṛhadaśva's instruction to King Janamejaya on the origins and sacred order of the Kashmir valley. It recounts the draining of the primeval lake by Viṣṇu and the establishment of the Nāga Nīla's worship, and prescribes the valley's tīrthas (pilgrimage sites), festivals, and ritual calendar, incorporating indigenous Nāga and Piśāca traditions.
history: The Nīlamata is the principal early source for the religious geography and customs of Kashmir, drawn on heavily by Kalhaṇa in the 12th-century Rājataraṅgiṇī. Scholars (e.g. Ved Kumari Ghai) date its core to roughly the 6th-8th century CE, with later interpolations; it was edited and translated in modern times by Ved Kumari Ghai.

title: Brahmāṇḍa-Purāṇa 1
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: core c. 4th-6th c. CE; reached present form c. 1000 CE (disputed)
summary: The first section (Pūrvabhāga / Prakriyā- and Anuṣaṅga-pāda) of the Brahmāṇḍa-Purāṇa, the Purāṇa named for its account of the 'cosmic egg' (brahmāṇḍa). This part sets the frame of the recitation and treats the classic Puranic topics of cosmogony, the measures of time and the kalpas, cosmography, and the manvantaras.
history: The Brahmāṇḍa is one of the oldest Mahāpurāṇas, closely related to (and once forming a unit with) the Vāyu-Purāṇa; its core is dated to roughly the 4th-6th century CE while the text is generally thought to have reached its present arrangement around 1000 CE (Rocher). It is famous for transmitting the Lalitā-māhātmya and Lalitā-sahasranāma in its later portion.

title: Brahmāṇḍa-Purāṇa 2
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: core c. 4th-6th c. CE; reached present form c. 1000 CE (disputed)
summary: The middle section (Madhyabhāga / Upodghāta-pāda) of the Brahmāṇḍa-Purāṇa. It continues the Puranic exposition with royal genealogies and dynastic accounts (vaṃśa and vaṃśānucarita), sage lineages, geographical lists, and supplementary mythological narratives.
history: Part of the Brahmāṇḍa-Purāṇa, one of the oldest Mahāpurāṇas, closely linked to the Vāyu-Purāṇa. Core dated to roughly the 4th-6th century CE with the present structure achieved about 1000 CE and continual later revision.

title: Brahmāṇḍa-Purāṇa 3
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: core c. 4th-6th c. CE; Lalitā material later (post-1000 CE); disputed
summary: The latter section (Uttarabhāga / Upasaṃhāra-pāda) of the Brahmāṇḍa-Purāṇa. It concludes the work and is celebrated for containing the Lalitopākhyāna (Lalitā-māhātmya), the story of the goddess Lalitā Tripurasundarī's destruction of the demon Bhaṇḍāsura, together with the famous Lalitā-sahasranāma, a centerpiece of Śrīvidyā goddess worship.
history: Part of the Brahmāṇḍa-Purāṇa; while the Purāṇa's core is early (4th-6th century CE), the Lalitā material in this section reflects later Śākta/Śrīvidyā tradition and is generally considered a comparatively late addition. The Lalitā-sahasranāma transmitted here is among the most widely recited goddess hymns.

title: Devīgītā [Devībhāgavata-Purāṇa]
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: Devībhāgavata c. 11th-12th c. CE (range 9th-14th c.); Devīgītā within it
summary: The Devī-gītā ('Song of the Goddess') comprises the final ten chapters of the seventh skandha of the Devībhāgavata-Purāṇa (7.31-40, c. 507 verses). It is a dialogue in which the Great Goddess (Mahādevī/Bhuvaneśvarī) reveals to the mountain-king Himavān her supreme nature as the source of the cosmos and teaches the path to union with her through knowledge, yoga, devotion, and her ritual worship.
history: The Devī-gītā is the Śākta counterpart to the Bhagavad-gītā, embedded in the Devībhāgavata-Purāṇa, a goddess-centered Purāṇa generally dated to roughly the 11th-12th century CE (scholarly range 9th-14th c.) that builds on the earlier Devī-māhātmya. It is studied and recited as an independent devotional-philosophical text in Śākta traditions.

title: Garuḍa-Purāṇa 2
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: core (Preta-khaṇḍa) c. 1st millennium CE, by c. 900 CE; whole work continually expanded
summary: The second book of the Garuḍa-Purāṇa, the Preta-khaṇḍa (or Dharma-kāṇḍa), dealing with death, the fate of the soul after death, the journey through the realms of Yama, the various hells, funerary and post-funerary rites (śrāddha), and the means of attaining a good afterlife. It is the section commonly recited in connection with Hindu death rituals.
history: Part of the Garuḍa-Purāṇa, framed as Viṣṇu's discourse to his mount Garuḍa and traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa. The Preta-khaṇḍa is regarded as the early core of the Purāṇa; the work as a whole is encyclopedic and was continually expanded through the early second millennium, with the core around the 9th-10th century CE. This eschatological section is the best-known and most-used part of the text in popular practice.

title: Garuḍa-Purāṇa 3
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: Brahma-khaṇḍa relatively late, by c. 10th c. CE; whole work continually expanded
summary: The third book of the Garuḍa-Purāṇa, the Brahma-khaṇḍa (or Mokṣa-kāṇḍa), which treats liberation (mokṣa), the nature of Brahman, devotion, and salvific knowledge. It is the shortest of the three sections and shows the influence of the Bhāgavata-Purāṇa.
history: Part of the Garuḍa-Purāṇa, traditionally ascribed to Vyāsa. Scholars regard the Brahma-khaṇḍa as the latest of the three sections because of its Bhāgavata influence, though still not later than about the 10th century CE; the Purāṇa as a whole is an encyclopedic, continually revised text.

title: Kūrma-Purāṇa 1
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
tradition: Hindu (Shaiva-Vaishnava synthesis; Pāñcarātra and Pāśupata material)
date_estimate: c. 7th-9th c. CE (core; range 6th-10th c., disputed)
summary: The first part (Pūrvabhāga) of the Kūrma-Purāṇa, narrated by Viṣṇu in his tortoise (kūrma) incarnation. It covers cosmogony and the churning of the ocean, cosmography and sacred geography, genealogies and manvantaras, devotional accounts of both Viṣṇu and the Goddess, and extensive tīrtha-māhātmyas (glorifications of pilgrimage sites such as Vārāṇasī and Prayāga).
history: The Kūrma-Purāṇa is notable for blending Vaishnava, Śaiva (Pāśupata), and Śākta material and is thought to have been reworked by Pāśupata redactors; its surviving form is generally dated to roughly the 7th-9th century CE. It survives in a recension significantly different from older quoted versions, indicating considerable revision.

title: Kūrma-Purāṇa 2
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Vyāsa)
date_estimate: c. 7th-9th c. CE (core; disputed)
summary: The second part (Uttarabhāga / Uttaravibhāga) of the Kūrma-Purāṇa. It contains teaching on dharma, the āśramas, and ritual, and is especially noted for embedding the Īśvara-gītā, an eleven-chapter philosophical discourse modeled on the Bhagavad-gītā but with Śiva as the supreme speaker, integrating Yoga and non-dualist Vedānta with Pāśupata theology.
history: Part of the Kūrma-Purāṇa, generally dated to roughly the 7th-9th century CE and associated with Pāśupata redaction. The Īśvara-gītā of this section is an important independent text for the philosophy of devotional Śaivism and the synthesis of Sāṅkhya-Yoga with Vedānta.
